en.extract:
The acacia tit (Melaniparus thruppi), also known as the Somali tit and northern grey tit, is a species of bird in the family Paridae. It is native to north eastern Africa where it occurs in dry acacia habitat.


Description
The smallest of the grey Melaniparus tits of Africa, the acacia tit has a glossy blue-black cap, nape, throat and breast contrasting with a large buffy white patch which extends from the bill to the sides of the neck. There is a broad black band which stretches from the breast to the vent. The upperparts are grey with white panels in the wings and there is a white spot on the nape. The underparts are greyish white, broken by the black band. Legs and bill are slate grey. The females tend to have a narrower band than males. Juveniles are similar to the adults but are duller. It measures 11.5–12 cm (4.5–4.7 in) in length and weighs 12 g (0.42 oz).


Distribution and habitat
The acacia tit is found from Ethiopia and Somalia south to north eastern Tanzania.
The acacia tit inhabits arid and semi arid wooded and bushed savanna, principally in acacia and stands of trees along streams or rivers, avoiding truly arid regions. It is recorded up to 2,000 m (6,600 ft) in Somalia.


Habits
The acacia tit is found in pairs or small parties and occasionally joins mixed species foraging flocks. The food consists of  small invertebrates, principally wasps, beetles and caterpillars. It nests in holes in trees and one nest found had been predated by a snake but otherwise the habits and ecology of this species are poorly known.


Taxonomy
The acacia tit was formerly one of the many species in the genus Parus but was moved to Melaniparus after a molecular phylogenetic analysis published in 2013 showed that the members of the new genus formed a distinct clade. It forms a superspecies with the southern grey tit and probably also the miombo tit and ashy tit. All of these species have been treated as conspecific with the Palearctic great tit but they are not now thought to be closely related to that species.


Subspecies
There are two currently recognised subspecies, they are:

Melaniparus thruppi thruppi Shelley, 1885: Ethiopia and Somalia
Melaniparus thruppi barakae Jackson, 1899: south western Somalia to Kenya, Uganda and north eastern Tanzania.


References

[[File:Somali Tit P1015390 DxO.jpg|thumb|Melaniparus thruppi]]
The '''acacia tit''' ('''''Melaniparus thruppi'''''), also known as the '''Somali tit''' and '''northern grey tit''', is a species of [[bird]] in the family [[Tit (bird)|Paridae]]. It is native to north eastern Africa where it occurs in dry acacia habitat.

==Description==
The smallest of the grey ''Melaniparus'' tits of Africa, the acacia tit has a glossy blue-black cap, nape, throat and breast contrasting with a large buffy white patch which extends from the bill to the sides of the neck. There is a broad black band which stretches from the breast to the vent. The upperparts are grey with white panels in the wings and there is a white spot on the nape. The underparts are greyish white, broken by the black band. Legs and bill are slate grey. The females tend to have a narrower band than males. Juveniles are similar to the adults but are duller.<ref name = "Zimmerman, Turner & Pearson">{{cite book | last1 = Zimmerman | first1 = Dale A. | last2 = Turner | first2 = Donald A. | last3 = Pearson | first3 = David J.| year = 1996 | title = Birds of Kenya and Northern Tanzania | publisher = Helm | page = 595 | isbn = 0-7136-3968-7}}</ref><ref name="Harrap & Quinn">{{cite book | last1 = Harrap | first1 = Simon | last2 = Quinn | first2 = David | year = 1996 | title = Tits, Nuthatches and Treecreepers | location = London, UK | publisher = Christopher Helm | pages = 345–347 | url = https://books.google.com/books?id=5_XUBAAAQBAJ&pg=PA347 | isbn = 0-7136-3964-4 | url-status = live | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20161220205545/https://books.google.co.uk/books?id=5_XUBAAAQBAJ&pg=PA347 | archive-date = 2016-12-20 }}</ref> It measures {{Convert|11.5–12|cm|in|abbr=on}} in length and weighs {{Convert|12|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name=HBW>{{cite web | url = http://www.hbw.com/species/somali-tit-parus-thruppi | title = Somali tit (''Melaniparus thruppi'') | access-date = 2016-11-06 | publisher = Lynx Edicions | work = Handbook of the Birds of the World | url-status = live | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160913081428/http://www.hbw.com/species/somali-tit-parus-thruppi | archive-date = 2016-09-13 }}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The acacia tit is found from [[Ethiopia]] and [[Somalia]] south to north eastern Tanzania.<ref name = HBW/>

The acacia tit inhabits arid and semi arid wooded and bushed savanna, principally in [[acacia]] and stands of trees along streams or rivers, avoiding truly arid regions. It is recorded up to {{Convert|2000|m|ft|abbr=on}} in Somalia.<ref name = "Harrap & Quinn"/>

==Habits==
The acacia tit is found in pairs or small parties and occasionally joins mixed species foraging flocks.<ref name = "Harrap & Quinn"/> The food consists of  small invertebrates, principally [[wasp]]s, [[beetle]]s and [[caterpillar]]s.<ref name = HBW/> It nests in holes in trees and one nest found had been predated by a [[snake]] but otherwise the habits and ecology of this species are poorly known.<ref name = "Harrap & Quinn"/>

==Taxonomy==
The acacia tit was formerly one of the many species in the [[genus]] ''[[Parus]]'' but was moved to ''[[Melaniparus]]'' after a [[molecular phylogenetic]] analysis published in 2013 showed that the members of the new genus formed a distinct [[clade]].<ref>{{ cite journal | last1=Johansson  | first1=U.S. | last2=Ekman | first2=J. | last3=Bowie | first3=R.C.K. | last4=Halvarsson | first4=P. | last5=Ohlson | first5=J.I. | last6=Price | first6=T.D. | last7=Ericson | first7=P.G.P. | year=2013 | title=A complete multilocus species phylogeny of the tits and chickadees (Aves: Paridae) | journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution | volume=69 | issue=3 | pages=852–860 | doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2013.06.019 | pmid=23831453}}</ref><ref name=ioc>{{cite web | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | title=Waxwings and their allies, tits & penduline tits | work=World Bird List Version 6.1 | url=http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/waxwings/ | publisher=International Ornithologists' Union | access-date=15 February 2016 | url-status=live | archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160219055004/http://www.worldbirdnames.org/bow/waxwings/ | archive-date=19 February 2016 }}</ref> It forms a [[superspecies]] with the [[southern grey tit]] and probably also the [[miombo tit]] and ashy tit.<ref name = HBW/> All of these species have been treated as conspecific with the [[Palearctic]] [[great tit]] but they are not now thought to be closely related to that species.<ref name = "Harrap & Quinn"/>

===Subspecies===
There are two currently recognised subspecies, they are:<ref name = ITIS/><ref name=Avibase>{{cite web | url = http://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=610FFD8E8CE34828 | title = Acacia tit ''Melaniparus thruppi'' Shelley, 1885 | access-date = 2016-11-06 | publisher = Denis Lepage | work = Avibase | url-status = live | archive-url = https://web.archive.org/web/20160410113748/http://avibase.bsc-eoc.org/species.jsp?avibaseid=610FFD8E8CE34828 | archive-date = 2016-04-10 }}</ref>

*''Melaniparus thruppi thruppi'' <small>Shelley, 1885</small>: Ethiopia and Somalia
*''Melaniparus thruppi barakae'' <small>Jackson, 1899</small>: south western Somalia to [[Kenya]], [[Uganda]] and north eastern Tanzania.
